person.name: Konrad Rzentarzewski
person.bio: I was there when the config burned. In the age before observability, chaos reigned. The servers howled, and nothing scaled but entropy. Then kube cattle were born — simple, nameless beasts born of children’s games. But they multiplied. And their hunger grew vast. Years passed. The sun revolved the Earth. Then the Earth revolved the Cloud. I grew tired of debugging in darkness. I longed for light. And logs. Preferably structured. That’s when I began preaching — not loudly, just… declaratively. One apply at a time. I walked among DevOps tribes, where bash begat shell, and shell begat pain. To them I brought ConfigMaps, and Secrets hidden in plain sight. Then came the mysterious YAML — a language too holy to lint — inscribed deep within the API’s own heart. Through toil and merge, the infrastructure became manifest. Pods floated on the waters. Deployments begat replicas. Helm bore Charts. Git begat Ops. What was once dev spun to prod. And every service had a name — and every name resolved. Unless external DNS was involved. And the Preachers said: \"Behold, our logs streameth clean.\" Thus began the age of the declarative. We once dwelled in darkness. Now, we scale planetary in full light.

He has also contributed to several CNCF-related projects, including serving as a reviewer for the system validator used in kubeadm preflight checks.

In 2023, he reviewed the Japanese translation of Learning eBPF, published by O'Reilly Japan. In 2024, he became an organizer of Cloud Native Community Japan (CNCJ), fostering open source culture in Japan. Additionally, he served as Co-Chair of KubeDay Japan 2024, playing a key role in shaping the event and promoting cloud-native technologies within the Japanese developer community.

person.bio: Sou um apaixonado por tecnologia, entusiasta do software livre e das alternativas abertas. Tenho 20 anos de experiência em infra estrutura on-premises e ultimamente tenho focado em me especializar em tecnologias voltadas para nuvem.

person.name: Ader Fu
person.bio: Ader Fu, a.k.a. Yao-De Fu has extensive experience in DevOps and works at Chunghwa Telecom Co., Ltd. I am an active member of the Cloud Native Taiwan User Group (https://www.facebook.com/groups/cloudnative.tw) and co-organized the Kubernetes Community Day meetup event gathering  (https://community.cncf.io/kcd-taiwan/)  provides expertise in Kubernetes and CNCF technologies. I am also a member of Kubernetes Special Interest Group (SIG) Docs, and serve as an approver in the open source project cncf/glossary, and as an approver and reviewer in kubernetes/website. I am now fully certified in Kubernetes (CKA, CKAD, CKS, KCNA and KCSA) and am the first Kubetronaut in Taiwan. Favorite technologies include Kubernetes/DevOps/CNCF/APIM, etc.

person.name: Jack Kelly
person.bio: General tech enthusiast. Free/Libre and Open Source Software advocate.  Jack Kelly has a strong interest in writing software, deploying it at scale, and doing so securely. Jack has a passion for nix and supports over 80 packages in nixpkgs, several of which being CNCF projects. With an interest in software delivery and nix it was natural for Jack to develop strong opinions around what constitutes a Secure Software-Supply Chain, and consults on such topics, in addition to facilitating as part of the In-Toto Steering Committee (ITSC).

person.name: Mateus Muller
person.bio: Mateus is a Senior DevOps Engineer at Zeta Global, where he uses CNCF tools and automation to enable developers to deliver good software, faster and more secure. Passionate about content creation, has also runs a blog and an youtube channel to share knowledge with Brazilian folks.  https://mateusmuller.me/ https://www.youtube.com/c/MateusMuller

My focus areas include container orchestration, infrastructure as code, and automation pipelines. I am committed to continuous learning and collaboration while contributing to the growth of the cloud-native community and exploring new ways to simplify complex distributed systems.

person.name: Ahmed Doban

person.name: Javan Zhao

person.name: Kerem İzgöl

In 2017, my book titled “Herkes İçin Tinylab ve Arduino” (Tinylab and Arduino for Everyone) was published by Abaküs Kitap. I am currently working as a Linux system administrator in Türk Ekonomi Bankası.

person.name: Maksym Troshyn

My journey as a kubeastronaut is powered not only by Linux Foundation certificates, but by AWS and HashiCorp certifications too. I am fueled by a love for AI and automation. Challenges excite me, innovation drives me, and obstacles simply don’t exist in my orbit.

My primary role involves designing and implementing cloud-native infrastructures across various SI (System Integration) projects. As a passionate advocate for container orchestration, I focus on building scalable and secure systems for enterprise clients. I am honored to join the Kubestronaut community and look forward to contributing to the ecosystem.
